speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went through-- through-- through-- through Salzburg. And then we stayed a few weeks in one of the port. But you know, the name of the ship was General Sturgis. All the ships were military ships. And I had the pleasure to sleep only one night in a regular, huge place for women only. And then the next day, they had a-- you know, every ship this has. They had a-- not excursion-- but they wanted everybody on the top-- you know, on the deck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, I was two or three-- two months pregnant. And every-- everything turned into me. And then one guy walked by, and he says, you must be pregnant. I said, how do you know? He says, because you are losing your baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he grabbed me to the Red Cross, because I didn't know what was where on the ship. He was a doctor. And I lost a baby. And I was-- the whole time-- in the hospital-- you know, in the ship's hospital. So my husband would bring me all kinds of frozen fruits. And he was very nice. Of course, he had to be nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, I think, 10 days-- something like that. And we ended up-- what I told you-- in-- what is the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Boston, yeah. I never believed that Boston is on the ocean-- on-- on the water. That's why I always think that I am mixing something up. And we got out from the ship. And there were lots of people from Jewish agency there. And we had our names and cards. So they knew we are from UNRRA or something like that. And they were very nice. Except they looked at one of our big, big baggage. And there was some set of-- it was a set of Rosenthal. And it was old. I bought it used once, and they didn't believe me. They said, this has to stay. We have to check. And you will have to pay. Which was wrong, because when we got here and the baggage came, the Jewish agency here told us, don't pay a penny. You are coming here for good. This is not that you are coming here with merchandise to sell and things like that. So this was OK. [LAUGHS] And it was very good, except that I was in the hospital and in pain. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't get sick when it's necessary to be well. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? So how was the determination made where you would go from Boston?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had tickets for Los Angeles. But that's how we travelled. Otherwise, we would have to wait for another month maybe, for a ship to New Orleans. Because that's the way we would travel normally.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also train. And people from the agency would show us-- this is your train. They were very nice. They took care of everything. And then here, also, people were waiting for us. And they had already a room for us. And everything was just perfect. And they took a taxi, and putted us in the taxi, and gave us money for taxi, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I were-- we were with a Jewish family. And when they saw the baggage that arrived later on with china, can you imagine? I was told oh, the poor Germans. You took everything away from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how you like that? Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I looked at her like crazy. And-- Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. [LAUGHS] Yeah. Matter of fact, we went through one of the castles in Salzburg. And there was lots of things from Hitler and his girlfriend, Eva Braun. And I was told I can choose what I want. And there was a piece, very big piece of black crepe for, you know, for an evening gown. So when I showed them that, just on purpose-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --she says, you did steal from her? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I had lots of things. You know, people did not know here really what it was. Like, we were invited to a neighbor for Thanksgiving. She takes me in the kitchen, and she says, see? America.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what she was showing-- that she had hot water. They thought that Europe was really very, very poor, whatever. But that's another-- went through their heads.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else? Then we got an apartment, because I got pregnant. And this apartment had kitchen-- a regular kitchen-- small kitchen, and a room that was during the day a living room, and in the-- somewhere in the closet was a bed that we pulled it out for night.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was OK. Don't forget, we are come-- we were coming from Holocaust, you know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything was great. Everything was great.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we met people right away. They lived around-- it was Adams and La Brea. That was the section.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time? Nothing. I was just pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did you eventually--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, actually I was pregnant-- I have two children. The first that was miscarried, it's-- you can't even say a child, because it was something but-- I was not very, very hurt about it-- just for a few-- few weeks or something like that. And then one-- somebody in pharmacy told me when the babies don't hold, then it's something wrong with them. So you're lucky that you got rid of it this way, you know. But we met lots of people here. We right away had groups of-- 1939 Club and so on. And in-- in 1951, of course, we couldn't-- I was pregnant. And nobody would rent an apartment or anything like that. So we were forced to buy something. And there was a three-- three-plex-- three units-- not even finished yet. But by the time we got ownership of it, there was only grass-- that they had to put new grass. It was a beautiful place-- one bedroom, one living room. And the kitchen had a-- a nice space for food-- for dinette or something like that. And Paulie was born in November 1951. And at that time, of course, every friend that wasn't pregnant came and helped me, because I shouldn't do this, and that, and so on. And she was born. She was a Thanksgiving girl. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Monica Henrietta. And she's now a cake decorator. She studied to be veterinarian. I don't know how it came to cake decorator. But when she was 16, she went we with me to a special place where they have wonderful decorated cakes. But the taste was good for nothing. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So she decided that she wants to be a cake decorator. And she's very, very artistic.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have two pictures here on the wall. And there are more pictures in the den. And she really does a very well job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what she does now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does your other daughter do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is in executive search-- completely different field, different girl. I have two completely different girls. One loves animals. And she likes to ride horses. And now she was here a few weeks ago to buy a Lassie-- collie. And she bought-- with papers and everything-- she took it home. She has a house with a whole acre of land.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on part, she has a horse and you know-- so that he cannot get out with all the trimmings. And now she has a little puppy that-- she brought it here for one night. If she would leave it another night, I don't think I would let her go--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --with it. So cute. So anyway, I am very happy with my girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What business was your husband in in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Officially, in Los Angeles, he worked for Standard Brand Paint for many, many years-- at least 10 years. And he saw that they are not you know, letting him to do other things to get up and up and make more money. Then he went into a-- a liquor store. And he had a partner there. And he was lucky. They were never hold up. So I was very angry. I didn't want him in liquor store. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in business. I worked for-- well, our friends were making petticoats-- bouffant petticoats. I-- I don't know if you know what I'm talking about. Do you know? Bouffant petticoat is a petticoat with ruffles that keeps you-- flares up-- gorgeous! And my friends couldn't make it. And before they bankrupt, they asked me if I could help them. I said, you know, bring me a few. I will sell maybe to the neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, no, you sell it to stores. Think about what stores. So I looked at him. I said, how about Sears and Roebuck? He says, you're crazy. My hair will grow--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --out here. Well, I did get Sears-- Sears and Roebuck, and Penney's, and all kinds of big stores-- and Bullocks Wilshire. And this was what I was so proud of-- [LAUGHS] --that I could have Sears and Roebuck and Bullocks Wilshire. And then I stopped. And-- oh, I forgot to tell you. In Innsbruck, before I came here, I learned Vienna Cosmetic School-- I had. So I decided to go into cosmetics-- skincare. And I made the license. And I was in skincare--

speaker: interviewee
text: --till-- till a few years ago. About two years ago I gave-- sold out everything-- all the equipment. And I was it in here, in my house. I gave lectures and stuff like that. But in '69, I joined ORT-- and right away in leadership. Because I worked for the school as a room mother-- and so on. So I knew lots of leadership and-- and things like that, until now.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got-- in '78 I became a president of my chapter-- in and out-- because every four years you have to change. And then in '91, I believe, I became a chairman of the executive committee for the whole Los Angeles region. It's a full mouth, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] And-- and then after one year I became a president for the whole thing-- that I'm very proud. And I believe in ORT. And I went to all the conventions. And my chapter was thriving beautifully. Now most of the women are older. They cannot work as much as-- so I had to, little by little, take every-- every one part of the ORT-- running ORT. Finally, I found a very nice and good treasurer who came with me to the region. But now I needed her. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told her, listen, I was in the region, and you are working for the region. Now you have to help me in my chapter. And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as far my husband is concerned, I have to go back to it. He got Alzheimer's disease for 15 years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Another cry. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't need anymore, do you? When-- when did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: In March, '85. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then even with Alzheimer, he always recognized me. And he was very nice to everybody. And-- and I was nice to everybody, so they would be nice to my husband. [LAUGHS] And they were. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I wanted to. And I survived-- survived, I think, for something. And I did a lot. And I'm very happy with it. And now I can go whenever they want me to. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like this generation still to listen to us as long as we are alive. Because then they have-- each and every one of us has a different book to write. It's not that we all went through the same thing. We all went through hell, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think our generation-- I am very proud of our generation. And I tell you why. 90% of our people raised their children with university education, or higher education-- people that maybe were not on the same level. Their children are much, much higher. And I think this is wonderful. And they should stick to their husbands and stick to their wives. There's too much that goes on. The children-- the small children suffer more than anything else with their divorces.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can I tell them? Be good to yourself and to everybody around you. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This picture is actually in my grandparents' house. And from left to right, there is the youngest brother of my mother, Simon. Then next to him is the youngest sister of my mother, Hannah. And then there is Jacob, who was raised by his grandparents. Now here you see my grandfather. And this must have been in between '18 and '20-- 1918 and '20.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Krakow, in the house of my grandparents. Next in the back of him-- more or less-- towards the right is my mother standing over my father. They were not married yet. They are playing cards. And they tried for my grandfather to win so that he will like them very much, especially my father-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --so that they could eventually marry. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture. As you see, I am sitting with my husband, very much in love, looking at each other. And the person that is a little bit in the back of my husband is the rabbi. He was from Hungary. And he came to our hachshara and gave us the wedding. Now this was done in Innsbruck, in 1946-- September 8th. This is a little boy, Lord Fauntleroy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's was called the outfit that he's wearing. It was very modern. This is my brother's picture at-- this was done in Krakow, in our-- in our house. And he was between four and five--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1929. This picture was sent by the photographer without even asking my mother. He sent to Warsaw, where they had to choose the most beautiful baby in Poland. Now he was not under his regular last name. It was [NON-ENGLISH] from Krakow-- the most beautiful child in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture from Zavoya. That was a place where we had a scouts gathering for vacation. I think it was about four weeks vacation. And I was 12 years old-- or maybe 11 years old-- something like that. And I am in the right costume.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that my parents had make for my brother's bar mitzvah. And from left to right, this is my father, Henrich Spira, my brother, Julian Spira, myself, and my mother, Paula Spira.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is a picture of myself, my brother, Julian Spira, and my mother, Paula Spira. This picture was asked by my father to be sent-- to-- to be mailed to him. He was, at that time in 1940, he was in Paris with military forces-- Polish military forces. And it's in Krakow. And it was made by the same photographer that the bar mitzvah's pictures were made.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother. It must have been beginning of their marriage. Because it must have been around 1920 or something like that. And it was done in Krakow. And this is a picture that I loved very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this pictures was made in Innsbruck. And it was in 1946-- no. I'm sorry. This was before the war ended. So it was in 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was 19-- 18 or 19 years old. And this is it. I hoped you like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she explain about the stamp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a telegram that came from Auschwitz with the news that my brother passed away. And it ask my mother-- and father, of course-- he was not there-- said if they want to know some more they can talk to Gestapo and they will explain to them everything-- how he died-- which was, of course, not true. They said he had a heart attack. He was only 15 years old. And it is in December 23, 1940, in Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband and me. I would like to-- to say something about him. He was in Auschwitz. And he helped people very much. And this was his habit. And this was done about four years-- oh, no. It must have been done in '79, something like that. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in one of the night clubs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would put the date about 1979 or '78, something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my picture that was taken about two years ago where I was honoring three doctors at this party-- Dr. Michael Newman, Kate-- Katherine Newman, his wife, also a doctor, and Dr. Kaufman. Now it says City of Los Angeles, Certificate of Appreciation is hereby presented to Freda Teitelbaum, President, Women's American ORT Greater Los Angeles Region, June 27, 1993. And it was signed by Zev Yaroslavsky, Councilman, fifth district. And I am very proud to have something like that hanging on my wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my daughter, older daughter, Paula Rebecca Teitelbaum. And she's-- she always was our joy. My husband carried her night after night, because she had a colic. Now she's a big girl. And you see her when she is 35-- something like that-- or over 30. And this was taken, I think in Las Vegas. And that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger daughter. Her name is Monica Henrietta Teitelbaum. This was taken when she was about 38 years old. Even though she's younger than Paulie-- because I don't want to mix the dates-- I want you to know that Monica looks exactly like she did in high school until today. And I am very happy and proud of her. Thank you.
